parts.title: Part 1
parts.instructions: You will hear some short conversations. Choose the correct answer to complete each conversation.

questions.prompt: Conversation 1
options: a) They need to pay more attention.
b) Let’s make sure we take advantage of that.
c) I’m afraid I can’t tell you.
questions.correct: b) Let’s make sure we take advantage of that.

questions.prompt: Conversation 2
options: a) OK, I expect it's there now.
b) As far as I know, everything was correct.
c) I wouldn't say so... I find it really absorbing.
questions.correct: c) I wouldn't say so... I find it really absorbing.

questions.prompt: Conversation 3
options: a) Would it have to take that long?
b) It means everything to me, I can assure you.
c) In actual fact, it’s almost done.
questions.correct: c) In actual fact, it’s almost done.

questions.prompt: Conversation 4
options: a) In that case, I’ll get down to it this afternoon.
b) It’s just the logical conclusion, isn’t it?
c) Please, don’t change the subject!
questions.correct: a) In that case, I’ll get down to it this afternoon.

questions.prompt: Conversation 5
options: a) As a rule of thumb it takes about fifteen minutes.
b) As usual, that’s easier said than done.
c) As long as he doesn’t ask me something out of the blue!
questions.correct: c) As long as he doesn’t ask me something out of the blue!

questions.prompt: Conversation 6
options: a) You need to talk him out of it.
b) Would you rather I do it instead?
c) There’s little comfort to be had in that!
questions.correct: a) You need to talk him out of it.

questions.prompt: Conversation 7
options: a) Are you going for good?
b) In that case, count me in.
c) There’s no clear basis for that.
questions.correct: b) In that case, count me in.

parts.title: Part 2
parts.instructions: You will hear five conversations. Choose the correct answer.

questions.prompt: When the tutor asks to speak with Jessica,
options: a) she assumes it is about some unfinished work.
b) she says that she is free to meet him tomorrow.
c) she reminds him that she will be late for her next lecture.
questions.correct: a) she assumes it is about some unfinished work.

questions.prompt: Jessica accepts her tutor's offer to
options: a) look at her report immediately.
b) send her an example work schedule.
c) sign her up for some study skills sessions.
questions.correct: c) sign her up for some study skills sessions.

questions.prompt: How does the student feel about the letter she's received?
options: a) determined to find out why she got it
b) embarrassed because it's not the first letter she’s had
c) confused about why it was sent to her old address
questions.correct: a) determined to find out why she got it

questions.prompt: The university employee offers to
options: a) contact the student's bank.
b) check the office records.
c) speak to her family.
questions.correct: b) check the office records.

questions.prompt: What is the tutor aiming to achieve in this meeting?
options: a) to encourage Ned to think about his goals
b) to stop Ned repeating the same mistakes in his work
c) to persuade Ned to try working with others
questions.correct: c) to persuade Ned to try working with others

questions.prompt: Ned is aware that, in his work, he sometimes fails to
options: a) select the best points to make.
b) spend enough time on his research.
c) go to a wide enough range of source texts.
questions.correct: a) select the best points to make.

questions.prompt: What do they agree about face recognition technology?
options: a) It should be used more widely.
b) It might be useful in certain situations.
c) It’s something people need to be concerned about.
questions.correct: b) It might be useful in certain situations.

questions.prompt: What opinion do they share about the magazine?
options: a) The articles make you think.
b) The interactive section is best.
c) The topics it deals with have wide appeal.
questions.correct: a) The articles make you think.

questions.prompt: The woman’s initial reaction to the content of the article reveals her
options: a) wrong assumptions about the research findings.
b) lack of faith in the research methodology used.
c) frustration at the attitude of the researchers.
questions.correct: a) wrong assumptions about the research findings.

questions.prompt: The man feels that the experiment needs
options: a) to include other variables.
b) to reach a far wider audience.
c) to be repeated on a much larger scale.
questions.correct: c) to be repeated on a much larger scale.

parts.title: Part 3
parts.instructions: Complete the notes. Write short answers of one to three words.

questions.prompt: Tree detail
Legal protection
Tree category
Tree part
Locations
Document
People involved

parts.title: Part 4
parts.instructions: You will hear a conversation. Choose the correct answers.

questions.prompt: Tanya points out that when Dr Gorman first started thinking about man-made objects in space
options: a) she was in a job where her abilities weren’t being fully exploited.
b) she already possessed the tools she would need to study them.
c) she briefly experienced a small crisis of confidence.
questions.correct: b) she already possessed the tools she would need to study them.

questions.prompt: According to Ken, what mistaken belief did Dr Gorman start out with?
options: a) She was the only one who had thought of studying space artefacts.
b) She would be able to readily access most of the data she needed.
c) Her efforts would be fully backed by the relevant space agencies.
questions.correct: a) She was the only one who had thought of studying space artefacts.

questions.prompt: Tanya tells the story of the Indonesian satellite to illustrate
options: a) the fact that communication is a recurring theme in archaeology.
b) the international fashion for launching satellites in the 1970s.
c) the symbolic nature of certain man-made objects in space.
questions.correct: c) the symbolic nature of certain man-made objects in space.

questions.prompt: What point does Ken make when discussing the need for space archaeology?
options: a) Efforts are needed to prevent space becoming the realm of the privileged few.
b) The majority of people appreciate the benefits space exploration offers.
c) Archaeologists are restricted by having to work within rules set by government bodies.
questions.correct: a) Efforts are needed to prevent space becoming the realm of the privileged few.

questions.prompt: When NASA advertised for astronauts, Justin Walsh responded with
options: a) surprise at the space agency’s narrow-minded attitude.
b) annoyance at NASA’s unwillingness to question man’s place in outer space.
c) determination to get a humanities perspective included on the programme.
questions.correct: c) determination to get a humanities perspective included on the programme.

questions.prompt: Talking about the ISS, Ken and Tanya agree that it’s surprising that
options: a) its inhabitants were so alike from the outset.
b) nobody saw the value of studying everyday life there.
c) life on board seems to have been relatively unregulated.
questions.correct: b) nobody saw the value of studying everyday life there.

parts.title: Part 1a
parts.instructions: Choose the option that best replaces the word or words in bold.

questions.prompt: There's no question that as science and technology advance, the world as we know it will change in ways we cannot imagine.
options: a) matter
b) doubt
c) idea
d) issue
questions.correct: b) doubt

questions.prompt: The use of technology in education has increased enormously in the last decade and now impacts on many facets of academic study, such as online teaching and internet research.
options: a) conditions
b) senses
c) aspects
d) sides
questions.correct: c) aspects

questions.prompt: The research expedition encountered very changeable weather, a mix of cold, wet days and hot, sunny weather.
options: a) unpredictable
b) alternative
c) inaccessible
d) flexible
questions.correct: a) unpredictable

questions.prompt: The market researchers altered the data to make the product appear more popular than it actually was.
options: a) influenced
b) conducted
c) guided
d) manipulated
questions.correct: d) manipulated

questions.prompt: The main problem with designing smart artificial intelligence systems is that they could trigger an explosion of intelligence that would quickly surpass human intellect.
options: a) stand for
b) call upon
c) bring about
d) take up
questions.correct: c) bring about

questions.prompt: Following the 1988 games, the organisers eventually bowed to mounting pressure and decided to allow professional athletes to compete in most categories in future.
options: a) tore in
b) caved in
c) butted in
d) chipped in
questions.correct: b) caved in

parts.title: Part 1b
parts.instructions: Read the text and decide which option best fits each gap.
parts.passage:
The Power of Dance

Dance has always played a significant role in human society. Brain-imaging studies of newborn babies have shown that they can already perceive rhythm, whilst the ability to move in time to music has been observed in babies from the age of five months. From then (7), moving to music seems to make the individual feel good.

According to neurological studies, this is because the human brain is essentially a predictive machine that constantly guesses what’s going to happen next and then derives pleasure from (8) a successful prediction. A regular beat is therefore (9) satisfying because those predictions become much easier, and the feel-good factor is thereby increased.

That’s why moving to music can make people happy even if they’re alone. But dancing in the company of others takes things to the next (10). This is because the pleasure of social bonding is added to the (11), which goes some way to explaining why dance features so prominently in many traditional social gatherings and rituals, as well as playing such an important role in modern lifestyles.

questions.prompt: Gap 7
options: a) onwards
b) forwards
c) upwards
questions.correct: a) onwards

questions.prompt: Gap 8
options: a) getting
b) having
c) making
questions.correct: c) making

questions.prompt: Gap 9
options: a) highly
b) strongly
c) widely
questions.correct: a) highly

questions.prompt: Gap 10
options: a) phase
b) level
c) grade
questions.correct: b) level

questions.prompt: Gap 11
options: a) mix
b) blend
c) recipe
questions.correct: a) mix

parts.title: Part 2
parts.instructions: Use sentences A-H to complete the text. There are two extra sentences.
parts.passage:
Researching wildlife in an area of ocean likely to be affected by deep-sea mining

I’m on board a research cruise ship in the middle of the equatorial Pacific Ocean, the Clarion-Clipperton Zone, to be exact. My research team is one among many. Various groups are surveying the environmental condition of the seas, obtaining data for different organisations. We are sending a remotely operated vehicle 4.5 kilometres down to a flat abyssal plain never explored before.

As deep-sea biologists, we’re privileged to discover new species and habitats on these research cruises. And this trip is no exception. We are recording an anemone-like animal with three-metre tentacles, plus purple and yellow sea cucumbers and flower-like glass sponges. (12) It’s an understandable reaction for a marine biologist working in an area that may be forever changed within the next few decades.

As demand for metals increases, such resources are being sought in ever more remote places, and mining will soon be taking place in the deep ocean. We’re here to find out more about wildlife in an area of ocean likely to be affected.

There are three types of resources being sought from deep-sea habitats: polymetallic nodules on abyssal plains, polymetallic sulphides at hydrothermal vents, and cobalt-rich seamounts throughout the global ocean. Already, 29 exploration permits have been granted by the International Seabed Authority (ISA). (13) Mining is close to happening: exploration regulations are currently being prepared.

The data we’ve been collecting is ready. We return home to our laboratories to continue working on the samples. (14) For instance, we discover that there are at least 1,000 species of fauna, 90 per cent of them new to science, in a city-sized area in the eastern Clarion-Clipperton Zone.

Yet our discoveries are also a cause for concern. Deep-sea mining will result in local extinctions of many creatures, leaving little possibility for their quick re-establishment. (15) Mining will also disturb large areas of the seafloor significantly. There will also be major changes to the geochemistry of the ocean’s food webs. And there are bigger questions, too. The oceans are experiencing stress from all angles: pollution, fishing, nutrient loading and climate change. (16)

Despite this threat, the various endangered habitats have been greatly under-explored. We know little about the species there and how they might cope with the impact of mining activities. At least our three-month voyage has hopefully improved the situation. (17) Nevertheless, our research means that there will be a solid starting point to measure future change against.
options: A) These problems are long-standing and ocean-wide, and how mining will worsen them is not yet understood.
B) Failure to do this could potentially change the world's oceans beyond repair.
C) After all, there are many such benefits that the deep sea provides for humanity.
D) We may only have sampled the seafloor of one small part of the Pacific Ocean, it’s true.
E) This body authorises such activity across 1.5 million square kilometres of the world's seafloor.
F) Around vents, this could take decades or more; with nodules, millions of years.
G) However, as incredible as all this is, I can’t help but feel some sadness.
H) And initially, the results we get from this analysis are very exciting.

questions.prompt: Gap 12
questions.correct: G) However, as incredible as all this is, I can’t help but feel some sadness.

questions.prompt: Gap 13
questions.correct: E) This body authorises such activity across 1.5 million square kilometres of the world's seafloor.

questions.prompt: Gap 14
questions.correct: H) And initially, the results we get from this analysis are very exciting.

questions.prompt: Gap 15
questions.correct: F) Around vents, this could take decades or more; with nodules, millions of years.

questions.prompt: Gap 16
questions.correct: A) These problems are long-standing and ocean-wide, and how mining will worsen them is not yet understood.

questions.prompt: Gap 17
questions.correct: D) We may only have sampled the seafloor of one small part of the Pacific Ocean, it’s true.

parts.title: Part 3
parts.instructions: Read the four texts about electricity generation and storage. Which text gives you the answer?
parts.passage:
A
Zachary Favors, a graduate student at University of California Riverside, drew inspiration for replacing graphite with silicone at a battery’s anode while he was resting on the sand at the beach. Sand is primarily made up of quartz but concentrations vary depending on the deposit. Favors found a quartz-rich site in Texas, drew some samples and enlisted the help of two engineering professors. Together they milled the sand until it was extremely fine, before taking the granules through a purification process. The purified quartz was then mixed with salt and magnesium and heated. The salt acts like a heat absorber, while the magnesium removes the oxygen, leaving pure silicon instead. To test the resulting powder, the researchers built a coin-sized battery. Performance tests reveal the silicon-anode lithium-ion battery lasts up to three times longer than those using graphite, which could dramatically change how our smartphones and other battery-powered gadgets are employed.

B
Wind and solar power generation differ from conventional thermal power due to their intermittent supply; this type of renewable electricity generation is strictly weather dependent and cannot be regulated to match the demand for electricity. In general, there are four strategies to reduce the impact of this. First, flexible supply can counteract intermittent supply. Second, demand can be shifted over time. Third, energy storage can store surplus electricity in batteries or hydrogen. Finally, trade can even out mismatches between production and load across countries.

C
Currently, most batteries are made with lithium. These are expensive, have a large carbon footprint, and are incompatible with long-term power storage. In the Finnish town of Kankaanpää, a team of engineers has completed the first commercial installation of a battery made from sand that they believe can solve the storage problem in a low-cost, low-impact way. The system stores energy as heat in hundreds of tonnes of sand for use in the local heating system whenever there is a dip in renewable energy production. Authorities want to scale up the system.

D
According to research from Lane Clark & Peacock, enough renewable power to supply 800,000 British homes went to waste in 2020 and 2021 when the national grid asked wind farms to switch off. Curtailment occurs when wind energy is available but cannot be used due to transmission constraints. These include insufficient ability to transport energy to areas with greatest demand, or the risk of overloading equipment. Wind power has increased significantly, but investment in processes and infrastructure has not kept pace. The financial and environmental costs make a strong case for long duration energy storage facilities.
options: A
B
C
D

questions.prompt: Express some frustration with the way one potential power supply has been under-exploited.
questions.correct: D

questions.prompt: List the deficiencies of a particular type of battery.
questions.correct: C

questions.prompt: Explore various measures for offsetting unreliable green electricity production.
questions.correct: B

questions.prompt: Go through the stages required in the production of a substance used in batteries.
questions.correct: A

questions.prompt: Suggest that an improvement to everyday technological devices is on the horizon.
questions.correct: A

questions.prompt: Make reference to an international market for energy from renewable sources.
questions.correct: B

questions.prompt: Report a disparity between one nation’s wish to exploit green energy and the technological means to realise that aim.
questions.correct: D

parts.title: Part 4
parts.instructions: Read the article about language preservation and answer the questions.
parts.passage:
Why are languages worth preserving?

I met Ariep, the last speaker of Naati, on a beach on Malekula, an island in the South Pacific nation of Vanuatu. He fears that with his death, Naati will soon disappear. Naati’s predicament is not unique. Of the roughly 7,000 languages spoken on the planet today, 50 to 90 per cent are considered vulnerable to extinction by the century’s end. Should Naati’s fate concern the world?

I argue yes. As a linguist who’s worked with endangered language communities, I know that language loss is a critical problem. Consider the injustices and persecutions that have happened over history to people whose language has been forcibly taken from them. Today, despite scarce resources, indigenous communities are making huge investments in reclaiming their languages. Language is the glue that binds communities together. Language loss is a loss of community heritage, from oral storytelling to knowledge of plants and practices codified through words unwritten and untranslated.

The disappearance of a language may seem like an unfortunate loss only to the people involved. However, the impact for all of us is real and substantial. Understanding language is vital to understanding human cognition. Each language is a piece of the puzzle that we need in order to determine how language works in the mind.

Languages may appear to differ wildly from one another, but they’re all variations on a theme. The remarkable similarities across languages suggest that there is some cognitive capacity that underlies all human language. The goal of contemporary linguistics is to describe and model this system.

This research has real implications for our lives. The more we understand about how language functions, the better equipped we are to improve therapies for communication disorders and methods for language teaching. Research on sound patterns is used in speech synthesis software, while models of grammar aid artificial intelligence.

questions.prompt: What is the writer doing in the first paragraph?
options: a) preparing the reader for an uncomfortable truth
b) picking up on contradictions in certain opinions
c) conceding that others have a fair point
d) presenting some arguments he will go on to reject
questions.correct: d) presenting some arguments he will go on to reject

questions.prompt: What point is the writer making about the indigenous communities mentioned in the second paragraph?
options: a) They are not persuaded by claims about the usefulness of languages.
b) They have historically struggled to find a common voice.
c) The younger generations are more open to linguistic change.
d) Their sense of mistreatment is strongly reflected in their languages.
questions.correct: a) They are not persuaded by claims about the usefulness of languages.

questions.prompt: The writer suggests that speakers of both Mohawk and Lulamogi use language as a way of
options: a) explaining their ethical choices.
b) preserving elements of their culture.
c) expressing their innermost feelings.
d) committing themselves to a future action.
questions.correct: b) preserving elements of their culture.

questions.prompt: The writer uses the image of a puzzle in the fourth paragraph to highlight
options: a) the unusual range of connections that exist between languages.
b) the challenge that linguists face in recreating lost languages.
c) the difficulty of identifying a common ancestry for all languages.
d) the value of all languages to the study of linguistics.
questions.correct: d) the value of all languages to the study of linguistics.

questions.prompt: In the fifth paragraph, the writer makes the point that
options: a) many words are remarkably similar within language families.
b) the forms of a language aren’t as random and chaotic as they might seem.
c) grammatical rules are merely attempts to describe systematic patterns.
d) written language typically involves a stylistic change from spoken language.
questions.correct: b) the forms of a language aren’t as random and chaotic as they might seem.

questions.prompt: In the last paragraph, the writer is saying that
options: a) early researchers studied languages on the basis of their similarity to English.
b) indigenous languages are of particular interest to developers of digital technology.
c) the optimum language analysis would draw on the broadest range of language groups.
d) the discipline of psycholinguistics is the most fascinating branch of language study today.
questions.correct: c) the optimum language analysis would draw on the broadest range of language groups.

parts.title: Part 1
parts.prompt: Your university has a large cafeteria, serving a variety of hot meals. The cafeteria is now used only by staff. As part of a short course in English, you have to write a report on changing use of the cafeteria over time. In your report, describe the main trends, give reasons for these trends, and predict how use of the cafeteria is likely to change in the next five-year period.

parts.title: Part 2
parts.prompt: Read the following statement and write an essay about the topic. The internet allows the individual to feel part of a global online community. Some people believe that this community can bring diverse people together and help solve global problems. Others disagree, fearing that it just leads to disagreements that drive people further apart. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write about 250 words.
